worth_it_explanation: Revenue and fill numbers from an SSP drive yield decisions, partner comparisons, and the invoices a publisher reconciles against payment. When the SSP report disagrees with the ad server, the payment remittance, or another partner's report, every decision built on those numbers inherits the gap. This check is a reconciliation review of reports the publisher can already pull from Magnite and the ad server, so it costs reading and matching time rather than a rebuild, and the finding points to a concrete reporting or configuration fix.
intro: Magnite is a sell-side platform (SSP) that a publisher uses to monetize inventory, and the revenue and fill figures it reports are the basis for yield decisions, partner comparisons, and payment reconciliation. This subcategory is about whether those figures are accurate and comparable, not about the auction or floor mechanics that produce them. These checks confirm that reported revenue and fill reconcile against actual payments and the ad server, and that report dimensions and timezones are set so the numbers line up across the systems a publisher compares.

audit_checks.title: Reported revenue and fill are not reconciled against payments and the ad server
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Revenue and fill numbers that no one reconciles can drift from what the publisher is actually paid and from what the ad server counts. An unreconciled gap can hide real revenue leakage, a billing shortfall, or a counting error that compounds across every month it goes unchecked, so the inventory may be earning less than the reports claim with no record that surfaces it.

educate.base:
A sell-side platform (SSP) reports the revenue it credits a publisher and the share of bid requests it fills, and a publisher may read those figures as the truth. The figures are an estimate until they are checked against two other records. The first is the payment the publisher actually receives since the remittance is the money that arrives and the report is the SSP's accounting of what it expects to pay. The second is the ad server's own count since the ad server and the SSP each measure impressions at a different point in the ad-serving path and will rarely match to the impression.

Gaps between these records are normal up to a point, and the audit question is whether the gap is measured and within a band the publisher accepts. A common cause is the counting model. Industry measurement guidelines from the Media Rating Council (MRC) and the Interactive Advertising Bureau Technology Laboratory (IAB Tech Lab) set a 'count-on-begin-to-render' minimum for a valid impression, but systems in the chain can count at slightly different moments, which produces a steady percentage difference rather than an error. Other causes are real money. A discrepancy that grows month over month, a fill rate that does not match the impressions paid for, or revenue that the remittance never catches up to can point to leakage rather than a counting quirk.

Reconciliation is the practice that separates the two. A publisher pulls the SSP revenue and fill report, the matching ad server report, and the payment remittance for the same period, lines them up, and records the size and direction of each gap. A small, stable, explained gap is fine. A large, growing, or unexplained gap is the finding.

A few signs point to revenue and fill that are not reconciled:
  • No process exists that matches SSP-reported revenue against the payment actually received for the same period
  • SSP impressions and revenue are never compared against the ad server's own count, so the discrepancy size is unknown
  • A known discrepancy has no recorded cause and no band the publisher has agreed is acceptable
You may want to confirm that reported revenue and fill are checked against both the payment remittance and the ad server count on a regular cycle. One approach is to reconcile a recent month across all three records, record the gap for each, and treat any gap that is large, growing, or unexplained as something to investigate with the SSP.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, direct-sold and programmatic demand often run through the same ad server, so an SSP revenue figure that is not reconciled against the ad server can be double-counted against a direct line item or credited to the wrong source. You may want to reconcile the SSP report against the ad server by line item or inventory source so the programmatic revenue is attributed cleanly and the payment matches what the report claims.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, ad revenue is one income stream among several, so an unreconciled SSP report can quietly misstate the contribution of the inventory. You may want to reconcile SSP revenue against the remittance each period so the figure that feeds the site's overall revenue picture reflects money actually received rather than the SSP's pre-payment estimate.

learn_more.source_title: MRC, IAB Tech Lab, and MMA revised ad measurement guidelines (count-on-begin-to-render)

educate.learn_more_note: Magnite's detailed reconciliation and revenue reporting documentation is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Pull the SSP revenue and fill report, the matching ad server report, and the payment remittance for the same period, then line them up to see the size and direction of each gap.

magnite_console.label: Magnite reporting console
steps: Open the Magnite reporting console (the platform's reporting or analytics area, depending on the product the publisher runs) and build a revenue and fill report for a recent closed month.
Record total paid impressions, fill rate, and net revenue for the period as the SSP reports them.
Locate the payment remittance or statement for the same period and record the amount actually paid for that inventory.

ad_server_reporting.label: Ad server reporting
steps: Build a report in the publisher's ad server for the same period and inventory, broken out so the Magnite source can be isolated.
Record the ad server's impression count and revenue for that source.
Compare the SSP impressions and revenue against the ad server count and against the remittance, then record the percentage gap and its direction for each pair.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, for a recent closed period, the SSP-reported revenue and fill, the ad server count for the same inventory, and the payment remittance, then notes the gap and direction for each pair. A setup where the three records are reconciled, the gaps are small and stable, and any difference has a recorded cause passes. A setup where no reconciliation exists, or where a gap is large, growing, or unexplained, is a fail recorded with the period and the size of each gap.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the SSP revenue and fill, the ad server count, and the payment remittance for the same period above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} does not reconcile its sell-side platform (SSP) revenue and fill against the payment remittance and the ad server count, so the size of any discrepancy is unknown. Reported revenue and fill are an estimate until they are matched against money actually received and against the ad server's own count, and an unmeasured gap can hide a counting difference or real revenue leakage. {{voice_recommend}} reconciling a recent closed period across all three records, recording the gap and direction for each pair, and treating any gap that is large, growing, or unexplained as something to raise with the SSP. After the first reconciliation, repeating it on a regular cycle keeps the numbers trustworthy.

audit_checks.title: Report dimensions and timezones are not aligned across the systems being compared
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A report built on a different timezone or a different set of dimensions than the report it is compared against produces a gap that looks like a discrepancy but is only a mismatch in how the two reports were cut. The mismatch wastes investigation time chasing a difference that is not real, and it can mask a genuine discrepancy hiding underneath the apparent one.

educate.base:
Two reports can only be compared when they describe the same thing the same way, and that depends on the dimensions and the timezone each report is cut on. A sell-side platform (SSP) report and an ad server report for 'yesterday' describe different windows of time if one runs on Eastern time and the other on the platform's billing time since the day boundaries fall at different moments. The result is a steady gap that has nothing to do with revenue or fill and everything to do with the clock.

The timezone is the most common source of a false discrepancy. If the publisher's ad server reports in one timezone and the SSP reports in another, a fixed number of hours shifts from one day into the next in every comparison, which shows up as a consistent difference that never resolves. Aligning the timezone, or comparing a longer window where the edge hours matter less, removes the false gap. Reporting systems often expose the choice. Google AdSense, for example, lets a publisher run reports in the account's local timezone or in the billing timezone, and the same kind of setting exists across SSP and ad server reporting.

Dimensions matter the same way. A report cut by ad unit does not line up against a report cut by site, a report that includes house or direct-sold inventory does not match one filtered to programmatic only, and a currency difference makes two revenue figures incomparable until one is converted. The audit question is whether the reports a publisher reconciles are cut on the same timezone, the same dimensions, and the same currency, or whether an apparent discrepancy is just two reports measuring different things.

A few signs point to dimensions and timezones that are not aligned:
  • The SSP report and the ad server report run on different timezones, so the day boundaries do not match
  • The two reports are filtered or grouped differently, for example one includes all inventory and the other only a subset
  • Revenue is compared across reports in different currencies with no conversion applied
You may want to confirm that any two reports being reconciled share the same timezone, dimensions, and currency before treating a gap as a real discrepancy. One approach is to fix the timezone and filters on both reports to match, then recheck whether the gap shrinks to the expected counting difference or whether a real discrepancy remains.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, reporting is often broken out by section or placement, so a mismatch in how ad units are grouped between the SSP and the ad server can make two correct reports look like they disagree. You may want to align the grouping, for example reconciling at the site or inventory-source level rather than matching an SSP breakout against a different ad-server breakout.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site that may sell internationally, currency and timezone differences are easy to overlook because the audience spans regions. You may want to confirm both reports use the same currency and timezone before comparing revenue, so a gap reflects a real difference rather than an unconverted currency or a shifted day boundary.

learn_more.source_title: Local Time Zone Reporting (AdSense Management API)

investigate.intro: Check the timezone, dimensions, and currency each report is cut on, then confirm the two reports being reconciled share all three before treating any gap as a discrepancy.

magnite_console.label: Magnite reporting console
steps: Open the report settings in the Magnite reporting console and record the timezone the report runs on and the currency the revenue is reported in.
Note the dimensions the report is grouped and filtered by, for example whether it covers all inventory or a subset.
Record whether the timezone, currency, and dimensions match those of the ad server report it is being compared against.

ad_server_reporting.label: Ad server reporting
steps: Open the matching ad server report and record its timezone, currency, and the dimensions it is grouped and filtered by.
Set the ad server report to the same timezone, currency, and grouping as the Magnite report, or note where they cannot be matched.
Recheck the gap between the two reports after aligning them, and record whether it shrank to the expected counting difference or whether a real discrepancy remains.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the timezone, currency, and dimensions each report is cut on and whether the two reports being reconciled share all three. A setup where the compared reports use the same timezone, currency, and grouping, so a remaining gap reflects a real difference, passes. A setup where the reports differ on timezone, currency, or dimensions, producing a gap that is a mismatch rather than a discrepancy, is a fail recorded with the dimension that does not align.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the timezone, currency, and dimensions for each report above to see whether the two being compared are aligned.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the reports {{site_name}} reconciles are not aligned on timezone, currency, or dimensions, so an apparent discrepancy may be a mismatch in how the reports were cut rather than a real difference. A sell-side platform (SSP) report and an ad server report on different timezones describe different windows of time, and different filters or currencies make two figures incomparable until they match. {{voice_recommend}} aligning the timezone, currency, and grouping on both reports before treating any gap as a discrepancy, then rechecking whether the gap shrinks to the expected counting difference or whether a real difference remains underneath. Keeping the reports cut the same way each cycle prevents false gaps from recurring.
